coredata.dc:title: Effective construction of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites through a facile and efficient route
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Huadong
Sun, Hao
Ke Zhen, Du
Jing liang, Cheng
XiaoYu, Sun
coredata.dc:description: Monoclinic bismuth vanadate (m-BiVO4) is a well-known photocatalytic material. However, its poor charge transport and photogenerated electron hole rapid recombination limit the application of m-BiVO4. In this paper, we successfully constructed a novel copper-sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu, S-CQDs)/BiVO4 nanocomposite through a facile and efficient route, which significantly improved the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4. The physicochemical properties of Cu, S-CQDs and Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 samples were studied by various characterization. It was concluded from the degradation experiments that the photocatalytic degradation rate of rhodamine B reaches 99% in 60 min under natural light. The degradation curve compounds the quasi-primary kinetic model, and the kinetic coefficient of degradation is 0.07831 min−1, which is 5.37 times than that of pure BiVO4. Moreover, the free radical scavenging experiments reveal that the hole and superoxide radicals play a major role in the photocatalysis process. After four cycles, the activity of photocatalyst exhibits high stability, which is extremely important for the photocatalytic process. In the summery, the prepared Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4has many advantages, such as environmental-friendly, low cost and a wide range of applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4
Photocatalysis
Visible-light-responsive
Facile synthesis
RhB

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)TEM images of Cu, S-CQDs. (b), (d), (e) SEM image of the BiVO4 (c) Particle size distribution histogram of Cu, S-CQDs.
(a), (b) TEM image of the BiVO4, (c)∼(i) TEM image of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4.
(a) HRTEM image of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 (b) EDS element mapping of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 (c) Bi, (d) C, (e) O, (f)V, (g) content of each element.
FT-IR patterns of BiVO4 and Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4.
XPS images of Cu, S-CQDs and Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 (a) full spectrum, (b) Bi4f, (c) C1s, (d) O1s, and (e)Cu2p.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum; (b) (ahn)1/2 versus (hn) plot of BiVO4 and Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4; The PL technique is commonly used to study the compounding efficiency of photoexcited electron holes [71,72]. The weak PL peak intensity implies low compounding efficiency. From the photoluminescence spectra, it can be summed up that the fluorescence efforts of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 is significantly lower than that of bismuth vanadate alone, which indicates that the doping of carbon dots reduces the complexation rate of the charges, improves the electron-hole separation efficiency of the composites, and effectively enhances the photocatalytic performance of bismuth vanadate.
Photoluminescence (PL) of BiVO4 and Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4.
(a) UV absorption and excitation emission spectra of Cu, S-CQDs; (b) excitation spectra at different wavelengths.
(a) Transient photocurrent response; (b) EIS Nyquist plots.
(a) Catalytic results of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 (b) pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting diagram.
(a) Catalytic results with different catalyst dosages, and (b) pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting diagram.
(a) Catalytic results with different Solution concentration, and (b) pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting diagram.
(a) Catalytic results with different rotate speed, and (b) pseudo-first-order kinetic fitting diagram.
Catalytic experiments of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 with four cycling runs.
The XRD pattern of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 after four cycling runs.
Active species trapping experiments during the photocatalytic degradation of RhB.
The possible mechanism of RhB degradation under visible light.

head.ce:title: Effective construction of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites through a facile and efficient route

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Power Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monoclinic bismuth vanadate (m-BiVO4) is a well-known photocatalytic material. However, its poor charge transport and photogenerated electron hole rapid recombination limit the application of m-BiVO4. In this paper, we successfully constructed a novel copper-sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (Cu, S-CQDs)/BiVO4 nanocomposite through a facile and efficient route, which significantly improved the photocatalytic performance of BiVO4. The physicochemical properties of Cu, S-CQDs and Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 samples were studied by various characterization. It was concluded from the degradation experiments that the photocatalytic degradation rate of rhodamine B reaches 99% in 60 min under natural light. The degradation curve compounds the quasi-primary kinetic model, and the kinetic coefficient of degradation is 0.07831 min−1, which is 5.37 times than that of pure BiVO4. Moreover, the free radical scavenging experiments reveal that the hole and superoxide radicals play a major role in the photocatalysis process. After four cycles, the activity of photocatalyst exhibits high stability, which is extremely important for the photocatalytic process. In the summery, the prepared Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4has many advantages, such as environmental-friendly, low cost and a wide range of applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of industrial, water pollution has become a critical issue [1]. The includes the widespread use of Rhodamine B. It is a typical wastewater pollutant used in laboratory cell fluorescent stain, colored glass, characteristic fireworks and other industries, which can cause subcutaneous tissue sarcoma [2]. At present, a variety of methods are available for treating pollutants, such as adsorption, biological treatment, and advanced oxidation [3,4]. Among them, photocatalysis is a possible solution to eliminate organic pollutants in wastewater for its excellent performance in high efficiency, low energy consumption, non-toxic and harmless. Photocatalytic reactions occur on the surface of the catalyst and enable faster conversion of light energy into chemical energy, including excitation, redox reactions, and recombination [5]. Currently, TiO2 and ZnO are the most widely utilized photocatalysts in various environmental and industrial applications, owing to their robustness, availability, and effective catalytic properties. However, these materials face significant limitations due to their low visible light utilization rates [6–11]. Thus, it is crucial to find new and efficient visible-light-driven photocatalysts.

In recent years, the research of bismuth vanadate has attracted tremendous attention [12–15]. Bismuth vanadate is a green ideal photocatalyst [16–18]. With its relatively narrow band gap, it has demonstrated significant potential in visible-light-driven photocatalysis [19–21]. However, the poor electron mobility of bismuth vanadate and the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge limits its photocatalytic efficiency. Therefore, the modification of bismuth vanadate is essential to improve his photocatalytic performance [22]. Many researchers have improved the properties of bismuth vanadate through metal doping and other semiconductor coupling to improve charge separation and photocatalytic activity [23–26]. However, many of these modification methods are complex and inefficient. In this paper, we tried to enhance the photocatalytic activity of bismuth vanadate by doping carbon quantum dots, which is facile and efficient [27].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have drawn increasing attention in recent several decades due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, and good biocompatibility, which is applied in medical imaging technology, environmental detection and energy development [21,28–32]. Carbon quantum dots play a vital role in photocatalyst composites for the excellent sunlight capture ability, dimmable photoluminescence (PL), up conversion photoluminescence (UCPL) and efficient photoexcitation electron transfer [33–35]. These excellent performances can broaden the light absorption range of semiconductor catalysts, excite semiconductor catalysts to form more photogenerated electron hole, and greatly reduce the rapid recombination of carriers [36–38]. Nowadays, there are many reports about the use of carbon point modified catalysts to improve the photocatalytic performance of catalysts [33,39]. The modification of CQDs can greatly improve the photocatalytic performance of the catalyst [40,41]. For example: ZnO/CQDs heterostructure with enhanced photocatalytic properties and facile fabrication of N-doped carbon quantum dots modified SnO2 composites for improved visible light photocatalytic activity [42–46]. Several CQDs/TiO2 composites have already been synthesized using the deposition and hydrothermal methods to enhance catalytic performance [44,47]. However, the Properties of CQDs needs to be further improved due to its weak photocatalytic performance [48].

In this work, we prepared Cu, S-CQDs nanocomposite by a simple and economical hydrothermal method to enhance the photocatalytic activity of BiVO4. The samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to analyze the morphology and structure. We also conducted electrochemical tests and free radical bursting tests to analyze and discuss the photocatalytic mechanism and the role of CQDs in enhancing the catalyst's performance. Overall, our study offers an economically viable and environmentally friendly approach to developing new, high-performance catalysts.

Chitosan, bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), l-ascorbic acid (AA), copper sulfate pentahydrate, ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), Ethanol absolute, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), 1-4benzoquinone (BQ), ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid (EDTA, >99.5%), Silver nitrate solution, rhodamine B (RhB) and isopropanol (IPA, 99.7%) were obtained from Aladdin. Ultrapure water was used throughout this work. The upper reagents are analytically pure, and no further purification is required.

The Cu, S-CQDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method [49–51]. Firstly, 0.3 g l-ascorbic acid and 0.01 g of copper sulfate pentahydrate were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water. Then the solution was converted into a 50 mL PTFE lining and were sonicated for 10 min to help the dissolution process. Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 8 h. After cooling, the synthesized carbon dot solution was subjected to dialysis for 12 h through the 0.22 μm filter membrane after filtration and the water was changed 3 times during the dialysis process. The obtained carbon quantum dots were stored at 4 °C.

Bismuth vanadate was prepared by direct precipitation method using ammonium metavanadate and bismuth nitrate as precursor. Briefly, 1 mmol of Bi (NO3)3·5H2O and 1 mmol of NH4VO3 were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water and put on a magnetic stirrer to stir. The pH was adjusted with 1 mol/l sodium hydroxide to 8 after the mixture was homogeneous. After stirring for 1 h, the mixture was transferred to a 50 ml PTFE reactor and heated at 200° for 2 h. After cooling naturally, we take the lower precipitate by the centrifuge. Then it was dried in an oven at 70° and finally yellow powdered bismuth vanadate was obtained.

Cu,S-CQDs/BiVO4 were prepared by room temperature impregnation method. First, 200 mg of bismuth vanadate, 1 ml of carbon point solution, and 30 ml of anhydrous ethanol were added to 50 ml beaker. Next, the mixed solution was placed on a magnetic stirrer at 600 rpm for 3 h. At last, the obtained solution was centrifuged in a centrifuge at 4000 rpm, and then the upper layer of the clear solution was poured out and dried in an oven at 70°.

The morphologies of Cu, S-CQDs and Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 were investigated by using transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEITalosF200s). The chemical properties of materials were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The UV–Vis spectra were used to study the optical absorption performances on the T6 spectrophotometer. The crystalline phases were identified by X-ray diffractometer (XRD). Fluorescence and photoluminescence (PL) surveys were conducted by the FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were conducted on an FTIR spectrophotometer. The morphology of the materials was analyzed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, USA-FEI-Quanta 250 FEG)). The photocurrent of the materials was analyzed on the Chenhua-CHI660e electrochemical workstation.

A standard three-electrode system was used to study Photoelectrochemical properties. The photocatalyst suspension was loaded onto the FTO conductive glass by drop-coating to make the working electrode. The Ag/AgCl electrode and Pt plate were used as the reference and counter electrode. The electrolyte was 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 solution (pH = 7). The visible light source was provided by a 300 W Xenon lamp and tuning the light on and off every 20s.

Rhodamine B was treated as an objective pollutant to investigate the photocatalytic properties of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4. We put 50 mg of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 to 50 ml of rhodamine B solution at a concentration of 2 mg/l. It was mixed foe 30 min in the dark before light exposure to establish the adsorption resolving equilibrium. The photocatalytic reactivity of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 was studied by observing the photodegradation of the Rh B under visible-light (λ > 420 nm) illumination using a 300 W Xe lamp. The reaction suspensions were sampled every 10 min during the photocatalysis process. The supernatant was centrifuged to obtain the upper layer of the clear liquid. The Rh B solution was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer after centrifugation.

Superoxide radicals (. O2-), holes (h+), electrons and hydroxyl radicals (. OH) were captured by adding 1–4 benzoquinone (BQ), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), silver nitrate solution and isopropyl alcohol (IPA), individually to quest the active substances during photocatalytic process. The concentrations added were all 0.01 mmol L−1.

The morphological characteristics of Cu, S-CQDs and BiVO4 were observed by SEM and TEM. Fig. 1 (a) shows TEM image of the Cu, S-CQDs, from which it can be seen that the uniformly dispersed carbon quantum dots were successfully prepared in the form of spheres with a size distribution from 2 to 5 nm, with a mean diameter of 2.8 nm, and no agglomeration occurred. The good dispersion of the carbon quantum dots indicates that the carbon dots have good monodispersity and high stability. Fig. 1(b),(d),(e) shows the SEM of bismuth vanadate. From the figure, it can be concluded that the synthesized bismuth vanadate has a smooth surface with mostly rod-like particles. The morphological structure of bismuth vanadate can be further observed by TEM.

The TEM images of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 are shown in Fig. 2 (a)–(i), from which it is possible to derive that the carbon dots are successfully doped on this surface of bismuth vanadate and dispersed very uniformly. It can also be seen that the carbon dots did not change the original shape of bismuth vanadate.

Fig. 3 (a)–(f) show the mapping elemental analysis of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 and the mapping maps of C, O, Bi, and V elements are distributed evenly. The HRTEM indicated that the lattice fringe spacing of 0.3071 nm is assigned to the (121) plane of monoclinic bismuth vanadate [52]. Fig. 3(g) 11 shows the EDS diagram of the composite, which shows the percentage of various major elements in the composite, Bi, V, O, and C are 16%, 29%, 44%, and 9%, respectively, which can further indicate that the carbon dots are successfully doped into the bismuth vanadate.

FTIR analysis was done to understand the components and surface groups of Cu,S-CQDs [53]. Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of BiVO4 and Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4. The peaks were viewed for Cu-O at 481 cm−1 [54]. The 1500 cm−1 peak was attributed to the bending vibrations of the C=C [55]. And the 1093 cm−1 peak was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the C-S56. The characteristic band at 1637 cm−1 are attributed to C=O bending vibration which shows the presence of carboxylgroup [57]. Moreover, the peak at 1637 cm−1 indicates the formation of C=O vibrational bending proves the outcome of UV–Vis absorbance at 284 nm ascribed to the π-π∗ transition of aromatic Cu, S-CQDs. A band at 2147 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H stretching mode [58]. The broadband around 3300 cm−1 could be attributed to the hydroxy group and H-bonded OH stretch [59]. Thus, Cu, S-CQDs had a carboxyl group (C=O) and a hydroxyl group (O-H). This demonstrates the synthesized Cu, S-CQDs had the excellent water solubility property. By exploring the FT-IR spectrum of Cu, S-CQDs, the presence of Cu-containing and S-containing groups suggested that Cu and S have been doped into the Cu, S-CQDs.

The main absorption peaks of BiVO4 appeared between 650 cm−1–850 cm−1. These peaks are associated with the stretching vibrations of V-O, V-O-V [60]. The weak absorption peak at 477.70 cm−1 was related to Bi-O bond [61]. The absorption peak at 3431.62 cm−1 is related to O–H, whereas two characteristic absorption bands at 1644.61 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of C=O due to the assimilate of H2O and CO2 on the specimens surface under air atmosphere [62,63]. The FTIR spectra of BiVO4 and Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 were very similar, which may be attributed to the lower content of carbon quantum dots, which did not affect the internal structure of the catalysts.

The XRD pattern of BiVO4 and Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 were shown in Fig. 5 . The peaks appeared at 18.5°, 28.9°, 30.5° and 34.5° stand the crystallite planes of (011), (121), (040) and (200) respectively which is consistent with crystal structure of m-BiVO4 (JCPDS14–0688) [64]. The Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites have XRD patterns similar to those of bismuth vanadate nanomaterials, and the positions of the characteristic peaks are basically the same as those of pure bismuth vanadate, but compared with the diffraction peaks of pure BiVO4, the diffraction peaks of the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites have reduced intensities and widened half-peak widths, which may potentially be attributed to the presence of a coating of Cu, S-CQDs on the surface of BiVO4. No obvious diffraction peaks of carbon quantum dots were found in the composites, which may be because of the low content of Cu, S-CQDs on the BiVO4.

The elements constitute and surface structures of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 were investigated by XPS. The XPS in Fig. 6 (a) shows three typical peaks at 160.01, 161.29 and 529.62 eV, which belonged to bismuth (Bi), sulfur (S) and oxygen (O). Furthermore, there were two peaks at 285.39 and 941.47 eV, which were related to C1s and Cu2p. Fig. 6(b) indicates the binding energy positions of Bi: those of Bi4f7/2 and Bi4f5/2 were 159.0 eV and 165.3 eV, respectively [11]. The spectrum of C1s (Fig. 6(c)) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6 (C=C), 285.7 (C–S), and 288.4 eV (C=O) [56]. The O1s peaks at 531.8 and 532.9 shown in Fig. 6(d) can be associated with C=O and O–H, respectively [65]. The high-resolution Cu 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 6(e)) shows the presence of Cu in Cu-CQDs (932.5 eV) [66,67]. Therefore, it indicates the existence of Cu in Cu-CQDs. The above XPS analysis is consistent with the FTIR results, further proving the presence of surface groups on the Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4.

Numerous studies have shown that the catalyst's ability to absorb light can affect its photocatalytic activity, and that the light absorption ability and the separation of photogenerated carriers are the key factors determining the photocatalytic performance [68–70]. In this paper, optical characterization of the samples was carried out by UV–visible diffuse reflectance. UV–visible diffuse reflection can be used to study the utilization of different wavelengths of light by photocatalysts. As can be seen from the figure, the prepared bismuth vanadate exhibits significant visible light absorption. After doping with carbon dots, the absorption band of the sample extends to the visible region and the absorption of visible light is gradually enhanced, which is mainly due to the visible light response of Cu, S-CQDs, and improves the utilization of the sample for sunlight. The doping of carbon dots makes the bismuth vanadate forbidden band width slightly narrower, as shown by Fig. 7 . The optical characterization of the composites further concluded that the composite of carbon dots can efficiently enhance the photocatalytic capability of bismuth vanadate by Fig. 8 .

The optical properties of Cu, S-CQDs were represented by fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 9 (a) indicates the absorption and excitation emission spectra of Cu, S-CQDs and it is derived that there are absorption peaks at 270 nm. The strong absorption peak of Cu, S-CQDs at 270 nm represents the transition from n-π* of C=O bonds [37]. As seen in Fig. 9(b), the Cu, S-CQDs exhibit distinct fluorescence spectra at distinct wavelengths of excitation light, and the fluorescence reaches its strongest at 350 nm excitation light.

The separation of holes and electrons under photo exposure can be further investigated by electrochemical experiments, such as transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy [73]. Under five intermittent light switching cycles, all the samples produced fast and stable photocurrent responses and it can be seen from Fig. 10 that the photocurrent of the composites doped with carbon dots is significantly higher than that of pure bismuth vanadate. It suggests that the doped carbon dots effectively enhance the separation efficiency of the photogenerated carriers and the photocurrent response is more stable during irradiation by visible light and light shading. Meanwhile, the electrochemical impedance of the composites is smaller than pure bismuth vanadate, and the low impedance can accelerate the separation of carriers, which is conducive to the improvement of catalytic performance.

The photocatalytic activity of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 composites was analyzed by the degradation of RhB under visible light irradiation. The degradation rate of bismuth vanadate on RhB was only 60% for 1 h, while the degradation rate reached 99% for 1 h after doping with carbon dots. The carbon quantum dots had high fluorescence intensity and generated many photogenerated electrons under visible light irradiation, and the electrons could easily migrate on the surfaces of the carbon quantum dots and the bismuth vanadate, which further enhanced the absorption of sunlight by the bismuth vanadate, and the CB of the bismuth vanadate of bismuth vanadate can be captured by the carbon dots, which can attenuate the rapid complexation of carriers, thus improving the photocatalytic degradation performance of the samples.

From Fig. 11 , it follows that the catalytic performance of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 is better than that of simple bismuth vanadate. The photocatalytic degradation experiments were carried out by doping different amounts of carbon dots, respectively. It can be concluded that the degradation efficiency firstly increases and then decreases as the amount of the carbon dots increases, in which the composites doped with 1 mL carbon dots had the best catalytic performance. The introduction of carbon dots enhanced the light absorption capacity of the photocatalyst, improved the separation efficiency of the electron-hole and also effectively reduced the electron-hole complexation rate. Too few carbon quantum dots could not effectively inhibit the compounding of photogenerated carriers, and too many carbon quantum dots might affect the absorption of photon energy by bismuth vanadate and hinder the generation of photogenerated electrons. The experimental results were further analyzed by kinetic fitting using a quasi-primary kinetic model, as shown in Fig. 11(b). The different materials exhibited better fitting data and the reaction rate constants varied.

Next, the effect of catalyst dosage on photocatalytic performance was analyzed. The photocatalytic degradation experiments were performed using 40, 50, 60 and 70 mg of catalysts, monomer carbon dots, and catalysts. Fig. 12 showed that 60 mg of catalysts explained the best effect. When the catalyst dosage is too low or too high, the effect on the photocatalytic activity is unfavorable. It is too low that may reduce the attachment sites of the photocatalyst and the target pollutants and too high may occupy the active center of the reaction, which is unfavorable to the photocatalytic reaction.

We also observed that as the concentration of RhB changed it also affected the photocatalytic effect. The photocatalytic degradation experiments were performed by controlling the concentration of rhodamine as 1, 2, 3, and 4 mg/l. The results are shown in Fig. 13 that the photocatalytic activity firstly increases and then decreases as the pollutant concentration increases. When the pollutant concentration was too high, its photocatalytic activity decreased, which may be due to the decrease of its transmittance to light and the decrease of photo-utilization rate as the pollutant concentration increased.

In order to improve the efficiency of the degradation, the effect of different rotational speeds on the photocatalytic performance was explored. The photocatalytic degradation tests were performed by controlling the rotational speeds of 400, 600, 800 and 1000 rpm. The results are shown in Fig. 14 that it can make the catalyst contact with the reactants more fully as the rotational speed increases, which improves mass transfer efficiency and increases the degradation efficiency of the catalyst. The rotational speed was finally set at 1000 rpm n order to avoid the splashing of the solution for the high rotational speed.

The stability of the catalyst is an important index to judge whether it can be used repeatedly for many times. In this paper, the reusability of the catalyst was tested by four cycles of degradation of rhodamine B on bismuth vanadate composites doped with carbon dots. The remaining catalyst was obtained by centrifuging the catalyst at the end of the photocatalytic reaction, which was washed repeatedly with deionized water. It can be derived from Fig. 15 that the degradation rate of the composite material under visible light for 60 min was still as high as 95% indicating that the sample has high photocatalytic efficiency and stability in practical applications after five cycles of experiments. Furthermore, we characterized the crystal structure of Cu, S-CQDs/BiVO4 after four cycling runs. From Fig. 16 , it is observable that the catalyst's crystal structure remains intact.

The free radical scavenging experiments were carried out on the catalyst for the degradation of RhB in visible light to investigate the active substances in the photocatalytic process. Superoxide radicals, holes, electrons, and hydroxyl radicals were captured by adding 1–4 benzoquinone (BQ), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), silver nitrate solution, and isopropanol to the samples during the experiment. The results from Fig. 17 showed that the addition of 1–4 benzoquinone (BQ) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) decreased the photodegradation efficiency while the addition of silver nitrate solution and isopropanol did not affect the photodegradation efficiency. It was illustrated that superoxide radicals and holes were mainly involved in the photocatalytic reaction process. Therefore, the photocatalysis of bismuth vanadate composites with carbon dots can be hypothesized from Fig. 16. The mechanism of RhB dyes degradation (Fig. 18) is shown as follows. The photocatalyst was excited after absorbing the photon energy whose energy is greater than its band gap under the light and the electrons jumped to the conduction band from the valence band resulting in photogenerated electrons in the conduction band and holes in the valance. These photogenerated electrons and holes tend to recombine quickly and only a fraction of the charges are able to participate in the photocatalytic reaction. Importantly, after doping with carbon dots, we obtained the absorption band of the photocatalyst extends to the visible region and the absorption of visible light is gradually enhanced through the UV–visible diffuse reflectance. Meanwhile. from the photoluminescence spectra and electrochemical performance analysis, it indicates that the doping of carbon dots reduces the complexation rate of the charges, improves the electron-hole separation efficiency of the composites. Then the photogenerated electrons and oxygen in the water reacted to generate superoxide radicals, which are powerful oxidizing agents that efficiently oxidize the organic pollutants. The RhB decomposed into CO2 and H2O by the redox reaction occurred in the role of the holes and superoxide radicals .
(1) BiVO4 + hv → h+ + e−
(2)
e −
+
O 2
→
O 2
. −
(3)
Rodam
B +
O 2
. −
→
CO 2
+
H 2
O
(4) Rodam B+ h+ → CO2 + H2O

In summary, Cu, S-CQDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method, BiVO4 was prepared by a direct precipitation method, and the composites were synthesized by a room temperature impregnation method. Investigations on the optical and electrochemical properties as well as photocatalytic properties of the composites were performed. The results showed that the composites exhibited significantly higher photocatalytic properties than only BiVO4, which was mainly attributed to the crucial role of CQDs in the samples. It improved the light absorption intensity, promoted higher charge separation efficiency, improved the charge transfer efficiency, and inhibited the complexation of photogenerated carriers of the bismuth vanadate. The main active substances in the reaction were investigated by free radical burst test. Meanwhile, the cycling experiments also proved that the composites have excellent stability and recyclability. This study is low-cost, simple, and environmentally friendly, which provides the possibility of efficient removal of pollutants from water by carbon quantum dot-modified semiconductor catalysts.
There is no human or animal study in this article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of generative AI and AI assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work the author(s) have not used the AI and AI assisted technologies.

Huadong Liu: Investigation, Conceptualization. Hao Sun: Writing - review & editing, Writing - original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Du Ke Zhen: Investigation. Cheng Jing liang: Validation. Sun XiaoYu: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Foundation of HeNan Educational Committee (24A4700010).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel method in emerging environmental contaminants detection: fiber optic sensors based on microfluidic chips
Sci. Total Environ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yuan, Y.; Jia, H.; Xu, D.; Wang, J., Novel method in emerging environmental contaminants detection: Fiber optic sensors based on microfluidic chips. Science of The Total Environment 2023, 857.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation of rhodamine B in water by ultrasound-assisted TiO2 photocatalysis
J. Clean. Prod.
